Exploratory Data Analysis

Overview

This section presents an enhanced Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) of the 2024 IT job market dataset. We examine job posting distributions, salary trends, geographic patterns, and in-demand skills to build a comprehensive picture of the current hiring landscape.

Load libraries and data 
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ── Consistent Plotly Theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────
import plotly.io as pio

custom_template = go.layout.Template()
custom_template.layout = go.Layout(
    font=dict(family="Segoe UI, Roboto, sans-serif", size=13, color="#2c3e50"),
    title=dict(font=dict(size=20, color="#2c3e50"), x=0.5, xanchor="center"),
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    plot_bgcolor="#f8f9fa",
    colorway=["#3498db", "#e74c3c", "#2ecc71", "#f39c12", "#9b59b6",
              "#1abc9c", "#e67e22", "#34495e", "#16a085", "#c0392b"],
    xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#dee2e6", linecolor="#adb5bd"),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#dee2e6", linecolor="#adb5bd"),
    hoverlabel=dict(font_size=12),
    margin=dict(t=60, b=40, l=50, r=30),
)
pio.templates["job_market"] = custom_template
pio.templates.default = "job_market"

# ── Download & Load Data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
import subprocess, os

data_file = "job_market_data.csv"
if not os.path.exists(data_file):
    subprocess.run([
        "gdown",
        "https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1V2GCHGt2dkFGqVBeoUFckU4IhUgk4ocQ",
        "-O", data_file
    ], check=True)

df = pd.read_csv(data_file)
print(f"Dataset shape: {df.shape}")
df.head()

1. Dataset Overview

Dataset summary statistics 
print(f"Total job postings: {len(df):,}")

print(f"\nColumn names:\n{list(df.columns)}")
print(f"\nData types:\n{df.dtypes}")
print(f"\nMissing values:\n{df.isnull().sum()[df.isnull().sum() > 0]}")

Missing values visualization 
missing = df.isnull().sum()
missing = missing[missing > 0].sort_values(ascending=True)

if len(missing) > 0:
    fig = px.bar(
        x=missing.values,
        y=missing.index,
        labels={"x": "Missing Count", "y": "Column"},
        title="Missing Values by Column",
        color=missing.values,
        color_continuous_scale="Reds"
    )
    fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, coloraxis_showscale=False)
    fig.show()
else:
    print("No missing values found in the dataset.")

2. Job Posting Distribution

Top job titles 
# Detect the job title column (common names)
title_col = None
for col in df.columns:
    if any(kw in col.lower() for kw in ['title', 'job_title', 'jobtitle', 'position']):
        title_col = col
        break

if title_col:
    top_titles = df[title_col].value_counts().head(15)
    fig = px.bar(
        x=top_titles.values,
        y=top_titles.index,
        orientation='h',
        title="Top 15 Job Titles",
        labels={"x": "Number of Postings", "y": "Job Title"},
        color=top_titles.values,
        color_continuous_scale="Blues"
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"), coloraxis_showscale=False)
    fig.show()
else:
    print("Job title column not found. Available columns:", list(df.columns))
Job category / type breakdown 
# Try to find category or job type column
cat_col = None
for col in df.columns:
    if any(kw in col.lower() for kw in ['category', 'type', 'job_type', 'employment']):
        cat_col = col
        break

if cat_col:
    cat_counts = df[cat_col].value_counts()
    fig = px.pie(
        values=cat_counts.values,
        names=cat_counts.index,
        title=f"Distribution by {cat_col}",
        hole=0.4
    )
    fig.update_traces(textposition='inside', textinfo='percent+label')
    fig.show()

3. Salary Analysis

Salary distribution and trends 
# Detect salary columns
salary_cols = [col for col in df.columns if any(kw in col.lower() for kw in ['salary', 'pay', 'compensation', 'wage'])]

if salary_cols:
    sal_col = salary_cols[0]
    # Clean salary data if needed
    df_salary = df[df[sal_col].notna()].copy()

    if df_salary[sal_col].dtype == 'object':
        # Try to extract numeric values
        df_salary[sal_col] = df_salary[sal_col].replace(r'[\$,K]', '', regex=True)
        df_salary[sal_col] = pd.to_numeric(df_salary[sal_col], errors='coerce')
        df_salary = df_salary.dropna(subset=[sal_col])

    if len(df_salary) > 0:
        fig = px.histogram(
            df_salary, x=sal_col,
            nbins=40,
            title=f"Distribution of {sal_col}",
            labels={sal_col: "Salary ($)"},
            marginal="box"
        )
        fig.show()

        # Salary by job title (if available)
        if title_col:
            top_10_titles = df_salary[title_col].value_counts().head(10).index
            df_top = df_salary[df_salary[title_col].isin(top_10_titles)]
            fig2 = px.box(
                df_top, x=sal_col, y=title_col,
                title="Salary Distribution by Top 10 Job Titles",
                labels={sal_col: "Salary ($)", title_col: "Job Title"}
            )
            fig2.update_layout(height=500)
            fig2.show()
else:
    print("No salary column found. Available columns:", list(df.columns))

4. Geographic Analysis

Job postings by location 
# Detect location column
loc_col = None
for col in df.columns:
    if any(kw in col.lower() for kw in ['location', 'city', 'state', 'region', 'area']):
        loc_col = col
        break

if loc_col:
    top_locs = df[loc_col].value_counts().head(20)

    fig = px.bar(
        x=top_locs.values,
        y=top_locs.index,
        orientation='h',
        title=f"Top 20 Locations by Job Postings",
        labels={"x": "Number of Postings", "y": loc_col},
        color=top_locs.values,
        color_continuous_scale="Viridis"
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"), coloraxis_showscale=False, height=600)
    fig.show()

    # If salary data exists, show salary by location
    if salary_cols and sal_col in df.columns:
        top_loc_list = top_locs.head(10).index
        df_loc_sal = df[df[loc_col].isin(top_loc_list)].copy()
        df_loc_sal[sal_col] = pd.to_numeric(df_loc_sal[sal_col], errors='coerce')
        df_loc_sal = df_loc_sal.dropna(subset=[sal_col])

        if len(df_loc_sal) > 0:
            fig2 = px.box(
                df_loc_sal, x=sal_col, y=loc_col,
                title="Salary Distribution by Top Locations",
                labels={sal_col: "Salary ($)", loc_col: "Location"},
                color=loc_col
            )
            fig2.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=500)
            fig2.show()
else:
    print("Location column not found. Available columns:", list(df.columns))

5. Skills Demand Analysis

Most in-demand skills from job postings 
from collections import Counter
import re

# Define common IT skills to search for
target_skills = [
    "Python", "SQL", "Java", "JavaScript", "R", "Scala", "C++", "C#",
    "AWS", "Azure", "GCP", "Docker", "Kubernetes",
    "Machine Learning", "Deep Learning", "NLP", "AI",
    "Tableau", "Power BI", "Excel",
    "Spark", "Hadoop", "Kafka",
    "Git", "Linux", "Agile", "Scrum",
    "TensorFlow", "PyTorch", "Scikit-learn",
    "MongoDB", "PostgreSQL", "MySQL", "NoSQL",
    "ETL", "Data Pipeline", "Airflow",
    "React", "Node.js", "HTML", "CSS",
    "Communication", "Leadership", "Problem Solving"
]

# Find description/skills column
desc_col = None
for col in df.columns:
    if any(kw in col.lower() for kw in ['description', 'skills', 'requirement', 'qualification', 'detail']):
        desc_col = col
        break

if desc_col:
    skill_counts = {}
    text_data = df[desc_col].dropna().str.lower()

    for skill in target_skills:
        count = text_data.str.contains(re.escape(skill.lower()), case=False, na=False).sum()
        if count > 0:
            skill_counts[skill] = count

    skill_df = pd.DataFrame(
        sorted(skill_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True),
        columns=["Skill", "Count"]
    ).head(20)

    fig = px.bar(
        skill_df, x="Count", y="Skill",
        orientation='h',
        title="Top 20 Most In-Demand Skills",
        color="Count",
        color_continuous_scale="Teal"
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"), coloraxis_showscale=False, height=550)
    fig.show()
else:
    print("Description/skills column not found. Available columns:", list(df.columns))

6. Correlation & Summary Statistics

Summary statistics for numeric columns 
numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number])
if len(numeric_df.columns) > 1:
    print("Descriptive Statistics:")
    display(numeric_df.describe().round(2))

    # Correlation heatmap
    corr = numeric_df.corr()
    fig = px.imshow(
        corr,
        text_auto=".2f",
        title="Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Features",
        color_continuous_scale="RdBu_r",
        aspect="auto"
    )
    fig.update_layout(height=500)
    fig.show()
else:
    print("Not enough numeric columns for correlation analysis.")

8 rows × 38 columns

Key Findings

Based on the exploratory analysis above, several patterns emerge:

  • Job Title Trends: The distribution reveals which IT roles dominate the 2024 hiring landscape.
  • Salary Insights: Salary distributions vary significantly across roles and locations, with certain specializations commanding premium compensation.
  • Geographic Hotspots: Major tech hubs continue to lead in posting volume, but remote opportunities are reshaping geographic dynamics.
  • Skills in Demand: Programming languages (Python, SQL) and cloud platforms (AWS, Azure) consistently rank among the most requested skills.

These findings inform the Skill Gap Analysis in the next section.
